content: We can frame LoCoMo (low-cost locomotion for robotic systems) as a design template for building embodied biological and chemical agent models, where 'locomotion' is redefined as the capacity of an agent to actively exert power to produce targeted effects in its environment. This reframing shifts agent design from focusing on pure reactivity to prioritizing low-cost, autonomous movement through an agent's native interaction space, whether that is a physical terrain for robots or a molecular signaling network for biological agents.
